entries.citation: kapustin-fidkowski — Kapustin & Fidkowski, "Local Commuting Projector Hamiltonians and the Quantum Hall Effect", Commun. Math. Phys. 373, 763 (2019/2020), arXiv:1810.07756
entries.notes: Web-verified this session (WebSearch + WebFetch of arXiv abstract + ar5iv full text). Abstract: 'We prove that neither Integer nor Fractional Quantum Hall Effects with nonzero Hall conductivity are possible in gapped systems described by Local Commuting Projector Hamiltonians.' Full-text fetch shows the actual argument: on a finite torus, assuming an on-site U(1) symmetry, the flux-dependent ground-state projector defines an algebraic vector bundle over (C*)^2, necessarily trivial, forcing the Chern number to vanish; the authors flag the thermodynamic-limit Hall-conductance conclusion as conditional on that limit existing.
entries.explanation: One of the six sources specifically flagged for spot-verification, and the load-bearing citation for Theorem V-C, the paper's central no-go. The headline attribution (CP Hamiltonian => zero Hall conductance) is correct and precisely what this paper proves. HOWEVER: the proof narrative given in the reviewed paper ('the commuting structure makes the relevant two-form (the adiabatic curvature integrated against flux insertions) exact, so its period vanishes... Their argument shows...') does not match Kapustin-Fidkowski's actual technique (algebraic-geometric triviality of a vector bundle over (C*)^2, contingent on an on-site U(1) symmetry not mentioned in the reviewed paper's Theorem V-C statement). The adiabatic-curvature/exact-two-form framing is instead the approach of the separately (and correctly) cited Kapustin-Sopenko paper (kapustin-sopenko-hall), a later and more general treatment. See that entry for detail. Net: correct paper, correct headline result; imprecise/conflated mechanism description that could mislead a reader checking 'what does the cited paper actually show'.

entries.citation: kapustin-sopenko-hall — Kapustin & Sopenko, "Hall conductance and the statistics of flux insertions in gapped interacting lattice systems", J. Math. Phys. 61, 101901 (2020), arXiv:2006.14151
entries.notes: Web-verified this session (WebSearch). Confirms: Hall conductance for zero-temperature infinite-volume 2D gapped short-range-interacting lattice systems is locally computable and identical throughout a gapped phase; gives a rigorous flux-insertion (Laughlin-type) argument that it is an integer (even integer, for bosonic SRE systems) multiple of e^2/h.
entries.explanation: The citation itself is valid and its 'locally-computable current response' description in the reviewed text is an accurate summary of this paper's actual contribution. The concern (see kapustin-fidkowski entry) is that the reviewed paper's Theorem V-C proof paragraph attributes the exact-two-form/adiabatic-curvature mechanism grammatically to Kapustin-Fidkowski ('their argument'), when that mechanism is this (separate, later) Kapustin-Sopenko paper's contribution rather than the 2018 Kapustin-Fidkowski paper's own (distinct, earlier, algebro-geometric) technique. Recommend the proof paragraph be reworded to clearly separate 'the theorem is Kapustin-Fidkowski's' from 'one way to see why, in the more general adiabatic-curvature language, is Kapustin-Sopenko's account.'

entries.explanation: Correctly cited as the rigorous lattice avatar of the higher Berry class / pi_1 pump invariant; standard, appropriate use.

entries.explanation: One of the six sources specifically flagged for spot-verification, newly added for this Part V. Confirmed to be exactly Kitaev's canonical 'Unpaired Majorana fermions in quantum wires' paper, matching both the bib entry's metadata and its use in the reviewed paper (1+1d class-D/BDI Kitaev chain, Z2 invariant, unpaired Majorana zero modes, topological for |mu|<2|t|). No issue found.

entries.explanation: Used only as an appropriately hedged ('conjecturally') identification of a candidate abstract target spectrum; non-load-bearing, accurate.

entries.citation: ogata-classification-review — Ogata, "Classification of gapped ground state phases in quantum spin systems", Proc. ICM 2022, arXiv:2110.04675 (2021)
entries.notes: Web-verified this session (WebSearch + WebFetch + ar5iv full-text fetch). Confirmed to be an ICM 2022 proceedings SURVEY: its own Theorem 6.1 states only 'There is a H^2(G,U(1))-valued invariant for one-dimensional SPT-phases' (existence, not completeness); a completeness statement ('a complete invariant of pure beta-invariant split states...') appears elsewhere in the survey but is attributed there to a different internal reference tag ([O5]-type), not proved fresh in this survey.
entries.explanation: FLAGGED ISSUE. Theorem V-B's proof states the d=1 completeness result is 'proved in \cite{ogata-classification-review}'. My verification shows this ICM survey itself points to a separate original paper for that specific completeness proof -- almost certainly Ogata's "Classification of symmetry protected topological phases in quantum spin chains" (Current Developments in Mathematics, 2021; arXiv:2110.04671), which is already present in this project's own references.bib under the key `ogata-spt-chains` (with a bib comment explicitly noting this exact same distinction) but is NOT cited anywhere in bordism-realizability.tex. The underlying physics claim (H^2(G,U(1)) is a complete 1D SPT invariant) is correct and is indeed a theorem of Ogata's; only the specific 'proved in' pointer is a secondary survey rather than the primary source, when the primary source is already verified and sitting unused in the shared bibliography. This same issue was independently identified by the citation reviewer of the companion Part IV paper (lattice-eft-equivalence-citation.json), which strengthens confidence that this is a real, reproducible finding rather than an artifact of one reviewer's search. Recommend citing \cite{ogata-spt-chains} directly for the d=1 claim in Theorem V-B's proof.

entries.citation: ogata-h3-index — Ogata, "A H^3(G,T)-valued index of symmetry protected topological phases with on-site finite group symmetry for two-dimensional quantum spin systems", Forum Math. Pi 9, e13 (2021), arXiv:2101.00426
entries.notes: Web-verified this session (WebFetch of the literal arXiv abstract, reproduced verbatim: 'We consider SPT-phases with on-site finite group G symmetry beta for two-dimensional quantum spin systems. We show that they have H^3(G,T)-valued invariant.'). Secondary sources (WebSearch) independently describe this as proving the nu=2 case of the physicists' H^(nu+1)(G,T) conjecture.
entries.explanation: One of the six sources specifically flagged for spot-verification. Correctly used as the primary source for the d=2 half of Theorem V-B (unlike the d=1 half, which relies on a survey -- see ogata-classification-review). The bare abstract text asserts existence of the invariant rather than spelling out 'complete invariant' verbatim, but this is very plausibly abstract terseness rather than an absent result: completeness is widely described in secondary literature as part of this paper's content, and a short two-sentence abstract for a technical operator-algebra paper would not necessarily restate every qualifier. Treated as adequately supported at moderate-high confidence; would benefit from a direct read of the paper body to close out with full certainty, which was not accessible via automated fetch this session.

entries.citation: walker-wang — Walker & Wang, "(3+1)-TQFTs and Topological Insulators", Front. Phys. 7, 150 (2012), arXiv:1104.2632
entries.notes: Web-verified this session (WebSearch + WebFetch of arXiv abstract + secondary literature). Abstract confirms a generalization of Levin-Wen models to (3+1)d based on unitary braided fusion (premodular) categories, with ground-state degeneracy on 3-manifolds and point/loop excitation statistics. Independent secondary sources (errorcorrectionzoo.org and others) describe the resulting Hamiltonian as a commuting-projector 3D topological code, trivial in the bulk (i.e. invertible) exactly when the input category is modular, with the chiral UMTC realized at the boundary.
entries.explanation: One of the six sources specifically flagged for spot-verification. Confirms Proposition (Walker-Wang realizations) accurately: commuting-projector Hamiltonian from a premodular/braided fusion category, closed-3-manifold fixed-point ground state, and -- when the category is modular -- an invertible bulk with chiral 2d boundary. Matches the reviewed paper's Prop. ww and the chiral-boundary-caveat Remark closely. No issue found.

entries.citation: xiong-minimalist — Xiong, "Minimalist approach to the classification of symmetry protected topological phases", J. Phys. A 51, 445001 (2018), arXiv:1701.00004
entries.notes: Not independently re-fetched this session; consistent with the known paper.
entries.explanation: Co-cited with gaiotto-jf for the generalized-cohomology framing; same appropriate contextual use.

missing_references: No citation is attached anywhere to the claim (Sec. 3.2, and again in the Discussion's 'Open directions') that the E8 state and other chiral phases are gapped-realizable via 'coupled-layer and network constructions' / 'coupled-wire and network constructions'. This specific constructive claim is stated twice without any supporting reference (e.g. Teo-Kane-style coupled-wire constructions of chiral phases, or a review of network models), unlike essentially every other constructive claim in the paper, which is backed by an explicit \cite.

Kitaev's toric code ('Fault-tolerant quantum computation by anyons', quant-ph/9707021) and the Levin-Wen string-net models (cond-mat/0404617) are each named explicitly, twice, as examples of commuting-projector-realized topological order ('toric code, string-net / Levin-Wen, Walker-Wang' in the Discussion; 'topologically ordered models such as the toric code' in Sec. 2.4) but neither is ever cited anywhere in the paper or in references.bib, even though the co-mentioned Walker-Wang construction in those same sentences IS properly cited elsewhere in the paper. Low priority -- both mentions are brief illustrative asides, not load-bearing claims -- but inconsistent with the paper's otherwise careful citation discipline.

Ogata, 'Classification of symmetry protected topological phases in quantum spin chains', arXiv:2110.04671 (Current Developments in Mathematics, 2021) -- the precise primary-source proof of the d=1 H^2(G,U(1)) completeness theorem currently attributed to the terser ICM survey ogata-classification-review (arXiv:2110.04675). This exact paper is already present in the shared references.bib under the key `ogata-spt-chains` (with a bib comment flagging this very distinction) but is not cited by bordism-realizability.tex. See the ogata-classification-review entry above for full detail; this was independently also flagged by the companion Part IV paper's citation review.
summary: All 39 unique \cite keys used in bordism-realizability.tex resolve to entries in references.bib (verified programmatically via grep cross-check), and the compiled PDF (papers/pdf/bordism-realizability.pdf) contains no unresolved '[?]' citation markers when converted to text. Deep web verification (WebSearch/WebFetch) was performed on 8 load-bearing sources, exceeding the requested minimum of 6 and covering all 6 specifically named for spot-check: Kapustin-Fidkowski, Chen-Gu-Liu-Wen, Else-Nayak, Ogata's d=1 and d=2 completeness sources, Walker-Wang, and the newly-added Kitaev Majorana-wire paper (cond-mat/0010440), plus Kapustin-Sopenko's Hall-conductance paper and Kubota's Omega-spectrum paper. Every one of these is a real, correctly-attributed paper whose title, authors, venue, and arXiv ID match the bib entry. Four of the five companion-paper citations (Parts I-IV) were structurally confirmed against sibling .tex files in this repository with matching titles; the fifth (paperSynthesis, Part VI) is a forward reference to a not-yet-drafted synthesis paper, an expected artifact of the pipeline's current stage rather than a defect. Two moderate-severity precision issues were found, both concerning proof narratives rather than headline attributions. First, Theorem V-B's d=1 completeness claim is cited to Ogata's ICM 2022 survey (ogata-classification-review), which is itself a review that points to a different underlying paper for that specific completeness result; the primary source (arXiv:2110.04671) is already verified and sitting unused in this project's own references.bib under the key ogata-spt-chains, and the same gap was independently found by the companion Part IV citation review, corroborating it as a real, reproducible issue rather than a one-off search artifact. Second, Theorem V-C's proof narrative describes the Kapustin-Fidkowski no-go as following from an 'adiabatic curvature two-form is exact' argument, but a full-text fetch of the actual Kapustin-Fidkowski paper shows their technique is instead algebraic-geometric (triviality of a vector bundle over (C*)^2, contingent on an on-site U(1) symmetry and a thermodynamic-limit assumption not mentioned in the reviewed paper's theorem statement); the exact-two-form/adiabatic-curvature framing belongs to the separately and correctly cited Kapustin-Sopenko paper. In both cases, the headline attribution -- which paper proved which theorem -- is correct; only the finer-grained mechanism narrative blurs which cited source contributes which piece, which matters if a reader follows the citation expecting to find that exact argument. Two minor citation-completeness gaps were also found and are listed under missing_references: the repeated, uncited claim that chiral phases are constructible via 'coupled-layer/coupled-wire and network constructions', and the uncited (though only illustrative) mentions of the toric code and Levin-Wen string-net models. No fabricated, nonexistent, or clearly false citation was found anywhere in the paper.
